visualSignals: Given one rate, asked to find another

decisionTree.question: What quantities are changing and what rates are given/requested?
Is there a geometric relationship?
decisionTree.ifNo: Find an equation relating the quantities

steps.instruction: Read carefully and identify all variables and rates
steps.pitfall: Don't skip this - misreading rates is common
steps.check: List: what's given, what's asked for

steps.instruction: Draw a diagram and label all variables
Write an equation relating the variables
steps.pitfall: May need geometry (Pythagorean, similar triangles, volume formulas)

steps.instruction: Differentiate both sides with respect to time t
steps.pitfall: Use implicit differentiation - all variables are functions of t
steps.check: Every term has a d/dt or derivative factor

commonErrors.example: For x² + y² = 25, differentiate to get 2x(dx/dt) + 2y(dy/dt) = 0, THEN substitute

commonErrors.diagnosis: Forgot that variables depend on t
commonErrors.fix: Every variable gets a d/dt factor when differentiating
Negative rate means decreasing, positive means increasing
commonErrors.example: If volume is decreasing, dV/dt is negative

workedExamples.problem: A ladder 10 ft long rests against a vertical wall. If the bottom slides away at 1 ft/s, how fast is the top sliding down when the bottom is 6 ft from the wall?

solution.math: \text{Let } x = \text{distance from wall}, y = \text{height on wall}

solution.explanation: Find y at the instant in question

workedExamples.keyInsight: The Pythagorean theorem relates the variables; implicit differentiation with respect to time gives the rate relationship

workedExamples.problem: A spherical balloon is inflated at 100 cm³/s. How fast is the radius increasing when r = 5 cm?

solution.explanation: Differentiate with respect to t (chain rule on r)

workedExamples.keyInsight: Geometric formulas provide the relationship between variables

workedExamples.problem: A street light is mounted at the top of a 15-ft pole. A man 6 ft tall walks away from the pole at 5 ft/s. How fast is his shadow lengthening when he is 30 ft from the pole?

solution.math: \text{Let } x = \text{man's distance from pole}, s = \text{shadow length}

solution.explanation: Shadow is 2/3 of distance from pole

workedExamples.keyInsight: Similar triangles often provide the key relationship in shadow problems

questions.integral: A conical tank is filling at 10 m³/min. How fast is the water level rising?
Find the maximum volume of a box with given surface area
A circle's area is increasing at 5 cm²/s. Find dr/dt when r = 3
questions.explanation: Given rate of area, find rate of radius - related rates

problems.problem: The area of a circle is increasing at 10 cm²/s. Find dA/dt when r = 5 cm (actually find dr/dt).
A 13-ft ladder slides down a wall. If dx/dt = 2 ft/s, find dy/dt when x = 5 ft.
Water pours into a conical tank (height 10m, radius 4m) at 5 m³/min. Find dh/dt when h = 5m.
A man walks along a dock pulling a boat with a rope. If his hands are 10 ft above water and he pulls at 2 ft/s, how fast is boat approaching when 20 ft from dock?
Two cars start from same point, one going north at 60 mph, other east at 80 mph. How fast is distance between them changing after 2 hours?
problems.context: Distance problem with two moving objects

cheatSheet.oneLiner: 1) Draw diagram, 2) Write equation, 3) Differentiate with respect to t, 4) Substitute, 5) Solve

masteryCriteria.recognitionSpeed: < 10 seconds to identify related rates problem
selfCheckQuestions: Did I differentiate before substituting values?
Does my answer have the right sign (increasing/decreasing)?
